Tafseer:
The meaning of the letter 'Noon':
Just as 'Qaf' is the name of the Surah in 'Qaf. By the honored Qur'an' (Qaf, 50:1), here 'Noon' is the name of this Surah. According to the recognized rule of Arabic grammar, the subject has been omitted here, which we have unpacked in the translation. In the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, we cited the theory of Imam Farahi that initially these letters were proofs of meanings; although the knowledge of their meanings is no longer preserved, some letters still manifest meanings. Among the letters cited by our teacher in support of this claim, this letter is also included, which is still used in its ancient meaning (fish). Naming this Surah by this name is an allusion to the incident of Prophet Jonah (peace be upon him) who was swallowed by the fish; hence, at the end of the Surah, his mention appears by the title of 'companion of the fish' (sahib-il-hoot). In Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 87, he is also referred to by the title of 'Dhu-n-Noon', which carries the exact same meaning as 'sahib-il-hoot'.
The testimony of the pen to three claims: 'By the pen and what they write' - this 'By' is for an oath, and we have repeatedly shown that in the Qur'an, oaths are sworn to testify to a claim. Here, as claims, three matters are mentioned—as detailed further on—to prove which this oath has been sworn.
First, the opponents' calling you (the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) mad is a sign of their own loss of intellect. You are not mad, but by the grace of Allah, you are wiser than all the wise.
Second, the opponents' assumption that all your activities are short-lived and will soon vanish in the wind is a complete misunderstanding. For you, an uninterrupted reward is destined in both this world and the Hereafter.
Third, you possess a noble character; therefore, those who ignore you by regarding you as a poet, a soothsayer, or mad are inviting their own ruin.
To prove these claims, the Qur'an itself is presented as testimony in various places; therefore, context suggests that here too, 'By the pen and what they write' refers to the Qur'an itself. Indeed, it is narrated from Mujahid that 'the pen' refers to the pen with which the Holy Qur'an was being written, and 'what they write' refers to the Holy Qur'an itself.
Some aspects of the pen's importance: It should be noted that 'teaching by the pen' is among the great blessings of Allah Almighty, as He states:
'Recite, and your Lord is the most Generous, Who taught by the pen, taught man that which he knew not.' (Al-Alaq, 96:3-5)
(Recite, and your Lord is highly generous, Who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know).
The teachings delivered by the past prophets (peace be upon them) were in the form of oral teachings, which were extremely difficult to preserve. They would quickly become distorted and altered, or fall into oblivion. To protect the religion from this calamity, Allah Almighty taught human beings the use of the pen and writing, enabling them to receive written instruction instead of mere oral instruction. Thus, first of all, the Ten Commandments of the Torah were written and given to him on tablets. Then, the teachings of other prophets were also penned down, and finally, with the greatest care, the final Book of Allah Almighty was preserved in such a manner that not the slightest possibility of alteration or change remains until the Day of Resurrection.
What is meant by the pen: Because of this importance of the pen, Allah Almighty has sworn by it here. In our view, it does not refer to any specific pen here, but represents the entire written record (WRITTEN RECORD) of divine teachings preserved through the pen—namely, the Torah, the Psalms, the Gospel, etc. The teachings of these holy scriptures also testify to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and contain undeniable testimonies of his appearance. All these matters have been clarified in their relevant places.
'and what they write': Context indicates that 'and what they write' refers to the Holy Qur'an, which was being revealed at that time and was being written by the hands of the companions. After swearing by the previous scriptures, this is an oath by the Qur'an itself. It is presented as a proof of the Prophet's truthfulness, composure, and messengership, showing that the person presenting such a noble and superior discourse is himself a proof that he is not a soothsayer, a poet, or mad, but the Messenger of Allah.
It should be noted that in response to such taunts from the disbelievers, Allah Almighty has generally presented the Qur'an itself to them, urging them to look at it and decide impartially whether this can be the speech of a madman, a soothsayer, or a poet, or of Allah Almighty.

The subject of the oath:
This is the subject of the oath. That is, all past heavenly scriptures and this Qur'an, which is being written, bear witness that by the grace of your Lord, you are not mad. Rather, you are warning people of the very things that every prophet from Adam (peace be upon him) to Jesus (peace be upon him) warned of, and to the truth of which history bears witness. If these claimants of wisdom call you mad for this crime, do not grieve; you are not mad, but are blessed with the greatest favor of your Lord, whereas the intellect of these sellers of wisdom is ruined, as they are unable to distinguish between the mad and the wise.
The reason for calling the Prophet mad: We have explained in its relevant place the reason why they called the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) mad—namely, that the leaders of the Quraysh could in no way understand where the punishment he warned them of with such emphasis and certainty (as if he saw it with his own eyes) would come from. They were concerned that his extraordinary certainty, his unparalleled restlessness in delivering the call, and his heart-shaking compassion and mercy in his reminders were influencing their common people. To neutralize this influence, they wanted to convince people that his restlessness was not because a punishment was actually coming and Allah had sent him to warn of it, but rather—just as some people suffer from a mania for something and keep repeating it constantly—this person has developed a mania for punishment, which he sees approaching from every side. To strengthen this, they would also add that someone had cast a spell on him, because of which his mental state was compromised and he was speaking incoherently.

Glad tiding of eternal success for the Messenger:
This explains the positive aspect, showing that foolish are those who regard you as mad and wait for some calamity of time to destroy you. Destruction is not destined for you, but for them. For you, indeed, is a never-ending reward, whereas the worldly goods given to the arrogant—upon which they boast—are now targets of punishment, the end of which they will see very soon. However, the recompense you are to receive for your commitment to the truth is eternal, which will never decline.
'Ghayra mamnun' means uninterrupted. Some have taken other meanings, but they are contrary to Arabic grammar and Qur'anic precedents.

The Messenger's character is a great proof of his claim:
That is, you are indeed a magnificent example of the noble models of character presented in the history of the prophets, and this character of yours is the greatest proof against those who call you mad, a soothsayer, or a poet, trying to convince themselves and their people that your words will vanish in the wind.
In the Holy Qur'an, the noble character of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is presented in various places as a proof of the truth of his claim. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, referencing the moral degradation, intellectual aimlessness, and inconsistency of words and deeds of soothsayers and poets, those who included the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in that wretched category are severely rebuked. They are asked: What connection does the noble character of the Prophet have with the character of those soothsayers and poets, whose outer and inner states are equally dark!

Glad tiding for the believers, warning for the opponents:
This is comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and a threat for the opponents: if they wish to devalue your words by calling you mad, then be patient for a few days. Soon you will see and they will see which party's reins are in the hands of a leader trapped in fitnah (trial)—the believers' reins, whom you are leading, or the Quraysh's reins, whom Abu Lahab and Abu Jahl are leading. The meaning is: the time of decision is near, and the manifestation of reality will not be delayed. Very soon, all will see who was trapped in the fitnah of Satan and led their nation into the pit of destruction, and who remained secure from the fitnah of Satan and guided his followers to the path of success in this world and the Hereafter!
Keep in mind the reality that we have repeatedly recalled—namely, that the divine law regarding the messengers is that when they come, they leave after deciding the fate of their group and their opponents in this very world. The comfort for the believers and the threat for the disbelievers in the verse relate to this world as well as the Hereafter. What was stated at the end of the previous Surah: (And you will know who it is that is in clear error) is repeated here in different words.

In 'Which of you is the afflicted' (bi-ayyikumu-l-maftun), the particle 'bi' apparently seems out of place with 'tubasiru' (you will see) and 'yubsirun' (they will see), but here a metaphor (tadmeen) is employed: 'yubsirun' implies the meaning of 'ya'lamun' (they will know). I recall that this was the opinion of Zamakhshari, and in my view, this opinion is in accordance with the principles of Arabic grammar. 'Bi-ayyikum' carries the meaning of 'bi-ayyi-l-hizbayn' (which of the two parties).
'Maftun' does not mean 'mad' (majnun), as some have assumed, but carries its literal meaning—namely, the one who is trapped in the net of the world and Satan. Using the word 'maftun' instead of 'majnun' here, the Qur'an indicates that those who are trapped in the net of the world and Satan are the truly mad ones, and the party whose reins are in the hands of such afflicted individuals ultimately ends up in Hell.

This supports and confirms the preceding theme: your Lord is neither unaware of those who have strayed from His path, nor is He ignorant of those who are guided; rather, He knows both of them well. He will deal with each according to what they deserve. It can neither happen that those who deserve disgrace remain forever in honor, nor that those who deserve success remain constantly targets of the wrongdoers' tyranny. This world is not an unjust kingdom; rather, it is created by a Great and All-Aware Creator. It is necessary that a Day of Justice comes. The meaning is: rely on your Lord; He will not treat the righteous and the wicked in the same manner.

That is, when the core reality is what has been explained, do not pay attention to the words of the deniers of the punishment and the Resurrection, and do not listen to their incoherent talk. If they are untroubled, believing there is neither punishment nor Resurrection, let them remain so. If they are satisfied that even if there is a Resurrection, they will receive the same there as they possess here, let them indulge in this sweet dream. This world does not revolve around the axis of their desires so that they receive whatever they wish; rather, it is created by a Wise and All-Mighty Being, and it is inevitable that one day His wisdom and justice manifest in their perfect form.
The word 'ita'ah' (obedience) here is used in the sense of being influenced by someone's words, which is a recognized usage in both classical Arabic and the Qur'an.

The actual policy of the opponents:
This reveals the actual cause behind the opposition of these deniers: their entire effort is to make you compromise in your stance so that they too may soften. That is, they have no doubt about the truth of your words, but accepting them is contrary to their desires; therefore, they have raised this storm to pressure you and make you compromise, so that you accept some of their ways and they accept some of yours, reaching a compromise on the basis of 'give and take'. The point is: their opposition is not based on any sincerity toward their pre-Islamic religion, but is merely an attempt at bargaining (BARGAINING). As long as they hope they can succeed in pressuring you, their attempt will continue. Once this hope is lost, they will lose courage.
Answering a grammatical question: A question arises regarding the grammar here—according to the rules of Arabic, it should have been 'waddu law tudhinu fa-yudhinu' [in the subjunctive], but it is 'fa-yudhinun' [indicative]. The answer is: a different style is adopted here, where the subject has been omitted—namely, 'fa-hum yudhinun' (so they will compromise). The meaning is: their desire is that when you compromise, they too will soften their attitude. Examples of this style exist in the Qur'an.

Moral portrait of the entire leadership of the Quraysh:
Linked to 'So do not obey the deniers', this warns with emphasis: do not listen to any habitual swearer of worthless character. This does not refer to a specific individual—as commentators have generally assumed—but portrays the moral degradation of the entire leadership of the Quraysh in the subsequent verses. The objective is to contrast the Prophet's matchless 'noble character' referenced in verse 4 with the character of the leaders of the Quraysh—such as Abu Lahab, Walid bin al-Mughirah, Abu Jahl, Akhnas bin Shariq, etc. By putting them side-by-side, any impartial person can decide who will face which end!
The fact that this portrays the character of the entire leadership, not a specific individual, is clear from several aspects:
First, it is linked to 'So do not obey the deniers', and 'deniers' (mukadhibeen) obviously refers to the entire leadership, as dictated by the context.
Second, the word 'kull' (every) proves that the discussion concerns the character of a group, not a specific individual.
Third, the subsequent words 'Indeed, We have tried them' (inna balawnahum) contain the plural pronoun 'hum' (them), proving that its reference is a group, not an individual.
Fourth, the character described here fits the entire leadership of the Quraysh perfectly, whereas trying to apply every single detail to a specific individual requires forced interpretation.
Keeping this in mind, reflect on the words:
'Hallaf' means one who swears oaths excessively. As explained in its relevant place, the word 'halif' is rarely used in a good sense, and here it is accompanied by the attribute 'maheen', which means worthless. Obviously, only a person who lacks self-respect will swear oaths excessively. Those who are morally degraded or compromised are always in doubt due to an inferiority complex, assuming the listener will not believe them unless they swear an oath; therefore, they swear oaths on every minor matter. Indeed, the Qur'an repeatedly states regarding the hypocrites that they rely on oaths to cover up their character. The leaders of the Quraysh had no room to criticize the character of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), nor any argument against Islam. To deceive the common people, their only support was to repeatedly swear oaths that he was a poet, a soothsayer, mad, and a fabricator.

'Hammaz' is the intensive form of 'hamz', meaning one who makes gestures or mocks. Making gestures and mocking is the characteristic style of those who wish to devalue someone in the eyes of others. This mocking is done through physical gestures of the eyes and eyebrows, as well as through words and phrases. The manner in which the arrogant leaders of the Quraysh mocked the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the poor Muslims is documented in the Qur'an, and clarified in its relevant places. The words: (Woe to every backbiter and slanderer) (Al-Humazah, 104:1) refer to this very character. The arrogant lack the power of argument; therefore, they use this cheap weapon to defeat the truth. But how can such weak arrows stand against the sharp sword of truth!
'Mashsha'im-bi-nameem' - 'nameemah' and 'nameem' mean backbiting and carrying tales. This refers to the habit of these dividers who spend their day and night in conspiracies, using backbiting as their tool. Whenever they want to sever someone's ties with another and align them with themselves, this is their greatest weapon.
They were using this same method to oppose Islam. Their constant effort was to spread baseless misunderstandings among the Muslims to cause division, so that the brotherhood and affection Islam had established among them would not strengthen, and to create doubt in people's hearts regarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).

Preventer of good, transgressor, and sinner:
The preceding verses showed that their leadership is built entirely on falsehood, mocking others, and backbiting. Now, it is clarified that they are active enemies of goodness, violators of Allah's limits, and encroachers upon the rights of His servants.
'Manna'il-lil-khayr' is general, meaning they are a heavy obstacle in the way of every goodness and benefit, but here it specifically points to their extreme miserliness—showing that they neither have the courage to spend a single penny to help the poor and needy, nor can they tolerate seeing others spend. Rather, they wish that others also remain miserly like them so that their own miserliness remains covered. In various places in the Qur'an, this aspect of the misers' character is clarified—showing that they encourage others to be miserly so that their own miserliness is not exposed.
'Mu'tadin atheem' - That is: not only do they not spend and prevent others from spending, but they also encroach upon the rights of others and dowerless withhold what is due from them. We have clarified the analysis of these two words in their relevant places: 'i'tida' contains the meaning of encroaching upon the rights of others, while 'ithm' contains the meaning of withholding rights.

'Utull' means hard-hearted and merciless. A person who is miserly will inevitably be hard-hearted as well. This represents the inner aspect of the character described above. Regarding such people, He states:
(Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan) (Al-Ma'un, 107:1-2).
'Zaneem': 'ba'da dhalika zaneem' - The lexicographers have explained 'zaneem' as:
(A person who attaches himself to a nation's lineage although he is not of them, and whom the nation does not need).
This word is derived from 'zanamah', which refers to the skin-tag or lobe that hangs from the neck of some goats, serving as a completely redundant part of the body. Narrations state regarding Akhnas bin Shariq that he was originally from Thaqif but claimed to be from Zuhrah. Similarly, it is well known regarding Walid bin al-Mughirah that he claimed to be of the Quraysh although he was not. Those who look down on their own lineage and try to insert themselves into another's lineage are boastful. Such people rely heavily on flattery, sycophancy, and a false display of national zeal to maintain their status in the community. Indeed, such compromised Qurashis were foremost in opposing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). To display their zeal, they would incite people against the Prophet, claiming his call was causing division in the unity of the Quraysh. Pointing to these boastful individuals, the Qur'an states: in addition to all the other vices they possess, they are also طفیلی (dependents) who claim to be more loyal to the nation than its actual members and serve as guardians of its pre-Islamic traditions. The Qur'an has dealt a blow to the character that inevitably develops in those who suffer from an inferiority complex and lack self-confidence.

The cause of corrupt character:
This explains why they developed such a character. It is stated: it occurred because they possessed wealth and children. This phrase is highly eloquent. The meaning is: when Allah blessed them with wealth and children, they should have risen as grateful, obedient servants and flag-bearers of the truth revealed by Him; but instead, they rose as ungrateful and corrupt individuals. In various places in the Qur'an, this reality is clarified: when Allah Almighty blesses people with His favors in this world, He tests them to see whether they become grateful servants or grow arrogant and become companions of Satan. Allah put these people to this test, but they failed completely, and Allah's blessing became a source of misery for them.

A portrait of pride and arrogance:
This portrays the pride and arrogance in which they were trapped. It is stated: when the accounts of past nations—who, like them, grew arrogant and were consequently destroyed—are recited to them, instead of learning a lesson, they mock them, saying: 'These are merely stories of the ancients; what connection do they have with the present?' The meaning is: we are neither ready to accept the one who recites such stories as a prophet, nor are we going to be intimidated by these stories. What does this have to do with accepting or rejecting anyone!

The punishment for arrogance:
This states the punishment destined for these arrogant individuals in the Hereafter. It is stated: very soon the time is coming when We will brand them on the snout. 'Khurtum' literally means a trunk (of an elephant). Here, it is used metaphorically for the noses of these arrogant individuals because they were opposing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) solely to keep their noses high (i.e., protect their pride). If a person develops such a mania for keeping his nose high that he commits himself to denying the most obvious truth for its sake, it means his nose is not just a nose, but has grown into a snout. It is stated: if they have turned their noses into snouts, let them do so; We will soon brand their snouts with disgrace for everyone to see. This is an excellent expression of arrogance and its punishment, the eloquence of which cannot be fully described.

A parable for the leaders of the Quraysh:
To expose the hollow nature of the leaders of the Quraysh described above, this parable is presented to them, demonstrating that the power and status they boast of—while mocking the warning of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—are built entirely on sand. Whenever He wills, He can reduce it to dust in the twinkling of an eye. At that moment, they will beat their heads in misery and pray for forgiveness, but their wailing will be of no benefit.
In 'We have tried them' (balawnahum), the reference of the pronoun 'hum' (them) is obviously the people whose character was discussed above. As noted, this is a clear indication that the character described concerns the entire leadership of the Quraysh, not a specific individual. If it concerned a single individual, the pronoun would have been singular.
Similarly, a grammatical point is worth noting here: in 'the companions of the garden' (as-haba-l-jannah), the word 'al-jannah' contains the definite article 'al', which might suggest a reference to the owners of a specific, historical garden. However, this is incorrect. In parables, the use of the definite article or relative pronouns like 'alladhi' and 'allati' is not meant—as we have clarified on several occasions—to refer to a specific, historical entity, but to specify and depict the situation vividly so that the reader gets a complete picture of the event. Therefore, there is no need to seek out the historical owners of a specific garden in Yemen or Sanaa—as commentators have done. Rather, this is a sketch portraying the mindset and the end of the leaders of the Quraysh so perfectly that no aspect remains hidden.
'when they swore that they would surely pluck its fruit in the morning' (idh aqsamu la-yasrimunnahu musbiheen) - This points to the confidence the owners of the garden had in their success. They were extremely secure and hopeful that their garden, having passed through all seasonal changes, had now reached a stage where it faced no threat of any calamity. In their view, the only task left was to go in the morning, pluck the fruits, and bring them home. Thus, they swore an oath that they would surely pluck its fruits in the morning.

A common misunderstanding:
Generally, people have interpreted this as meaning they did not say 'In sha' Allah' (if Allah wills) while swearing the oath. They regarded their success as so certain that they did not even conceive that any obstacle could arise. Although all commentators agree on this interpretation, it does not satisfy me. Although the word 'istithna' can accommodate this meaning, the style in which it is expressed here is not suitable for it. If that was the point, it should have been 'wa lam yastathnu' or some other similar style instead of 'wa la yastathnun'. Furthermore, the word 'istithna' itself is not an explicit term for saying 'In sha' Allah'; wherever it is interpreted as such, it is done with the help of context, and here the context is not so clear as to satisfy the mind completely.
In my view, 'wa la yastathnun' carries its literal meaning—namely, they swore they would pluck the fruits of their garden in the morning and would make no exception (i.e., leave nothing behind). The meaning is: 'We will not follow the practice of those who, when plucking the fruits of their garden, leave some portion behind for the poor and needy.' It should be noted that among pious and generous people, it has been a well-known practice since ancient times that when plucking the fruits of their gardens, they would leave a portion as the right of the needy. In the Gospels, this teaching of Jesus (peace be upon him) is recorded: 'When you harvest your garden, do not harvest it completely; leave some portion for the poor and needy.' Keeping this well-known practice in view, these miserly individuals swore that they would make no exception. They needed to swear an oath specifically to confirm this miserly resolve; otherwise, there was no need for an oath in the sentence.
Since this parable portrays the character of the leaders of the Quraysh described above as 'preventers of good, harsh, and sinners', the oath of the garden owners is specifically highlighted to demonstrate the complete similarity between the character of both. Recall also the depiction of the miserliness of Abu Lahab and his associates in various places in the Qur'an.

That is, having made the decision with great resolve, certainty, and oaths, they slept during the night; but while they were still asleep, a divine visitation from your Lord passed over their garden, turning it into a harvested field. In 'a visitation from your Lord' (ta'ifum-mir-rabbika), attributing the visitation to Allah Almighty points to two realities: first, that it appeared completely unexpectedly; second, its irresistible nature—showing that it turned a green garden into a barren land in the twinkling of an eye.
'from your Lord' (mir-rabbika) addresses the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) for comfort. In the initial verses, the Prophet was addressed and told: today you warn them of Allah's punishment, but seeing their outward circumstances stable and favorable, they call you mad. They cannot understand where the punishment would come from. In this parable, it is shown: when the punishment of your Lord comes, it comes in this manner—such that the planners make all their plans with oaths and sleep, but when they rise in the morning, they find:
'All that was seen and heard was but a dream.'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19 and 20 was combined in the original text.]

These people slept during the night after making the plan, and as soon as they rose in the morning, they called out to one another: 'If you want to pluck the fruits and harvest the crop, reach your fields early!' Although 'harth' literally means a crop or field, here it refers to the garden itself. The reason—as we have explained in its relevant place—is that in Arabia, portions within the gardens were used for cultivating various crops; therefore, they could be described as both a garden (jannah) and a crop/field (harth).
The words 'if you would pluck the fruit' (in kuntum sarimeen) are meant to urge and prepare the partners, meaning: if this task is to be done, do not waste time; go immediately, or you will suffer a loss.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21 and 22 was combined in the original text.]

That is, they departed, whispering to one another: 'Be careful, no poor person should enter the garden today!' This expresses their miserliness in other words, which was described above by 'making no exception'.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 23 and 24 was combined in the original text.]

The word 'hard' contains the meanings of rapid movement, determination, enthusiasm, and joy. The meaning is: having made full arrangements to avoid the poor, they went to the garden with great resolve and complete confidence. 'Qadireen' means their hearts were filled with confidence and resolve, thinking: 'What is there to fear now? The garden is ours, the fruits are ready, and who can interfere with our desires! We feared the poor might rush in, but we have prevented that as well.'

That is, when they looked at the garden, the celestial visitation had altered its appearance so completely that at first sight, they could not recognize it. They thought: 'Perhaps in the darkness, we have taken a wrong path.' They said: 'Indeed, we are lost!' But when the actual reality dawned upon them—that they had not lost the path, but the garden itself was devastated—they said with deep regret: 'Rather, we are deprived!' 'With what hopes and resolve we left our homes, but here there is nothing but dust!'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 26 and 27 was combined in the original text.]

'Awsatuhum' refers to the most moderate, balanced, and reasonable person among them. Even in the worst society, there are always some righteous souls who correct people for their deviation, whether the heedless listen or not. A similar servant of God was among them, who would occasionally remind them: 'Do not remain heedless of your Lord, but glorify Him.' The word 'tasbih' (glorification) is a comprehensive term that covers the entire meaning of remembering and worshipping Allah. At first, his advice had no effect on these intoxicated individuals; but when the consequence of their negligence appeared before them, only then did they realize that God is indeed a reality and this servant of God was right!

They immediately said: 'Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, He has not wronged us, but we were wrongdoers to our own souls, becoming so intoxicated by our success that we forgot His majesty; and when someone tried to remind us, we ignored it.' This is a confession similar to the one Pharaoh made when he was engulfed by the waves along with his army. Repentance of this kind, offered after the time has passed, is of no benefit.

When the consequence appeared, they began to blame one another. One blamed the other for not letting him choose the right path, while another accused his companion of preventing him from listening to the advisor. Those who do not use their intellect blame one another in this manner after seeing the consequence of their foolishness, although all of them are guilty. The only difference is that some open the path of corruption, while others follow them blindly. Ultimately, all must confess that everyone shared in the guilt.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30 and 31 was combined in the original text.]

That is, after blaming one another and confessing their failure, they also expressed the hope: 'We now turn in devotion to our Lord. We hope He will substitute for us a better garden than this.'
The Qur'an has not commented on their hope here, but the divine law is that when people repent after the opportunity has passed, their repentance is not worthy of acceptance with Allah Almighty.
The purpose for which this parable is narrated to the Quraysh is exactly the same as that of the parable narrated in Surah Al-Kahf, verses 32-43; refer to it to further clarify the subject.

Warning to the Quraysh after the parable:
After narrating the parable, this is a warning to the Quraysh that the punishment of which the Messenger is warning them will likewise fall upon them. Today, they are enjoying their luxury and are completely untroubled by God's grasp. The Messenger warns them of danger, and they call him mad. They cannot understand where the punishment would come from. It will come from where they least expect it, and at that moment, their state will be like that of the garden owners, but their wailing will be of no benefit.
In 'Such is the punishment' (kadhalika-l-'adhab), the 'punishment' refers to the punishment that, according to the divine law, has always befallen a nation when it rejected its messenger and the messenger had fulfilled his duty of communication. This punishment—as we have clarified in various places—decides the fate of that nation. After this, they will have to face the punishment of the Hereafter, which will be far greater. Allah's messengers warned their nations of both of these punishments.
'If they only knew' (law kanu ya'lamun) expresses regret and sorrow: to these blind-of-intellect, the Hereafter seems highly unlikely, whereas it is a solid reality and its punishment is extremely terrifying—if they only knew and understood.

The end of the righteous:
After describing the end of the arrogant, this states the reward of those who feared Him. It is stated: for those who lived their lives fearing the accountability of the Hereafter, there are gardens of pleasure with their Lord. This mention of the 'righteous' (muttaqeen) is in contrast to the arrogant, who were completely untroubled by God's grasp and the punishment of the Hereafter due to their worldly success. This contrast clarifies the actual meaning of the word: the righteous are those who do not lose themselves in the outward deceptions of this world, but keep their eyes on the reality hidden behind them.

Distinguishing between the righteous and the wicked is an essential requirement of divine justice:
This is the proof showing why there will be gardens of pleasure for those who fear God. It is stated: this is the essential requirement of His justice and mercy. If it were not so, it would mean that to the Creator of this world, the righteous and the wicked, the loyal and the rebellious, the unfaithful and the faithful are all equal. This is obviously contrary to the noble attributes of Allah Almighty. How can it be possible that in His sight, the good and the bad are equal?

Denying Resurrection and recompense is contrary to human nature and intellect:
This asks these arrogant individuals in amazement: What has happened to you? Where has your intellect gone that you render such judgments? The meaning is: if you deny the Hereafter and recompense, believing life is only the life of this world—that it will just continue like this or end one day without consequence—it means you regard its Creator as devoid of justice and mercy, having no concern with who led a life of goodness and who led a life of evil. If this is your judgment, consider how far it is from intellect and nature! What a major accusation you are leveling against the Creator of this universe, whose power, wisdom, providence, and mercy are testified to by every corner of this creation!
This style of questioning clearly shows that common human intellect and nature refuse to accept such a judgment. If some people still render such a judgment, it is due to one of two reasons: either they are helpless against their desires and are deceiving their own intellect and denying their nature, or they have completely ruined both of these faculties.

A blow to a delusion of the arrogant:
This deals a blow to another vain desire of the arrogant Quraysh. Their claim was: first, there is no such thing as the Hereafter; and second, if there is, we will receive better there than we possess here. This delusion was based on the assumption: 'If we were not approved by God, how would we have attained this honor and status in this world? Since we are approved, what we receive there will be greater than what we possess here.' It is asked: What has misled you into this delusion? Do you possess a scripture from God in which you read that all your desires will be fulfilled? The meaning is: the desires you harbor in your hearts have no foundation in intellect or nature; so if you possess a heavenly scripture in which these matters are written, present it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 37 and 38 was combined in the original text.]

It is asked: Or do you have oaths from Us, reaching to the Day of Resurrection, that you will have whatever you judge? Obviously, you cannot point to any such covenant; so what is it that makes you so boastful, believing that no one can shake you in this world and that you face no accountability in the Hereafter!
It should noted here that the promises of success in the Hereafter Allah has made to nations are conditional upon faith, righteous deeds, and observing the covenant He made with people through His messengers. He has not made any unconditional covenant with any nation that reaches to the Day of Resurrection. The covenant of leadership Allah made with both branches of the progeny of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them) is documented in both the Torah and the Qur'an, clarifying that this covenant does not apply to those who break God's covenant and wrong their own souls. When Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) passed the trial of sacrifice and Allah Almighty gave him the glad tiding of leadership:
(Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people) (Al-Baqarah, 2:124),
Abraham asked:
(And of my descendants?)
Allah Almighty immediately responded:
(My covenant does not include the wrongdoers).
This decision of Allah Almighty obviously applies to the Children of Ishmael just as it applied to the Children of Israel. However, the Quraysh, under the delusion of being descendants of Abraham and Ishmael, assumed they were free of any accountability before Allah, just as the Children of Israel grew arrogant, saying: (We are the children of Allah and His beloved) (Al-Ma'idah, 5:18), and assumed they were above any accountability in the Hereafter.

It is stated: ask them which of them will guarantee that God has issued an unconditional, eternal decree of success for them? If they have partners who they assume will not allow God to seize them, let them produce them, if they are truthful in their claim. That is, let them show them, or at least name them, so that others can also assess their status and reality. Note that regarding the idols and partners of whom the polytheists boasted—believing they were close to God and would save them from His grasp—the Qur'an has exposed their hollow nature so thoroughly that they could not dare to name any of them in response to this challenge.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 40 and 41 was combined in the original text.]

The meaning of 'kashf saq': 'kashf saq' (uncovering the shin) is a well-known Arabic idiom used to express the severity of a situation. The poets of Jahiliyyah used it in various ways. Hatim's famous verse states:
'A man of war, if war bites him, he bites it back; and if war uncovers its shin (i.e., rages fiercely), he too girds up.'
In this verse, 'shammarat 'an saqiha-l-harb' is used to express the fierce raging of war.
The origin of this idiom for expressing a severe situation is that when a major calamity or panic occurs, even noble ladies are forced to lift their garments to flee, uncovering their shins and their anklets. A poet says:
'It will make the old man forget his children, and will expose the shins and anklets of the noble virgins.'
The meaning is: these people are indulging in the sweet dream that they will remain in luxury in the Hereafter just as they are in this world, but that Day will be one of great panic. Today, they are invited to prostrate before God and they behave arrogantly, but on that Day, they will be called to prostrate, and they will wish to bend for prostration, but their backs will become rigid like wooden boards so that they will be unable to bend despite their efforts. Their eyes will be humbled, and humiliation will cover them. Their entire posture will bear witness to their disgrace and helplessness.
This command to prostrate will obviously be given solely to establish the proof and humiliate them, so that their own bodies bear witness to their rebelliousness and deprivation, which they cannot deny.
This same subject is described in Surah Al-Ma'arij in these words:
(The Day they will emerge from the graves rapidly as if they were hastening to a goal, their eyes humbled, humiliation covering them. That is the Day which they had been promised) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:43-44).
The same subject described in Surah Al-Qalam by 'the Day the shin is uncovered' is described in that verse by 'the Day they will emerge from the graves rapidly'; thus, both the language and Qur'anic precedents support this interpretation.
Based on a narration, some have interpreted it to mean 'the Day Allah Almighty uncovers His shin,' but several leading commentators have narrated the same interpretation we have adopted. It is narrated from Ikrimah and Ibn Abbas: 'It is the Day of Resurrection, a Day of distress and severity.' Ibn Jarir has also quoted a poet's verse supporting this meaning from Ibn Mas'ud or Ibn Abbas, which uses the idiom 'shalat-il-harbu 'an saqin'. The famous commentator Mujahid has also interpreted it in the sense of the severity of the situation.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 42 and 43 was combined in the original text.]

Warning to the deniers:
This is a warning to those who deny the Qur'an, whose mention appeared in verse 15:
(When Our verses are recited to him, he says, 'Stories of the ancients')
Alongside the warning, there is also great comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Addressing him, it is stated: leave the matter of these deniers of the Qur'an to Me. You have fulfilled your responsibility of communication. Now, step aside and let Me deal with them alone. I will lead them step by step to the pit of destruction from where they least expect. I am granting them respite, which they regard as their success, whereas they are caught in the net of death.

That is, during this gradual leading (istidraj), I grant them respite so that they may show their efforts and exhaust their strength. Extending their rope does not mean they can escape My grasp; indeed, My plan is extremely solid.

Rebuking the deniers for their deprivation:
This expresses amazement and rebukes them for fleeing from the Qur'an. The meaning is: why do they not listen to you? What does it cost them to listen? You are not asking them for any payment for communicating the message, such that they are burdened by it? This also contains comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): if they do not listen, do not grieve. If they are fleeing from this blessing, it is their own deprivation. You are sharing this heavenly wealth freely, gaining honor with your Lord.

This also expresses amazement at their heedlessness: on what basis are they ignoring this warning with such indifference? Do they possess the knowledge of the unseen, which they are writing down, asserting that they have high ranks in the Hereafter (if it occurs)? The meaning is: creating a fantasy of Paradise in the Hereafter based on desires without any proof or authority, while closing one's eyes to the realities of life, is not wisdom, but preparing for one's eternal ruin.
This verse has appeared in Surah At-Tur; we explained it there in the light of its context. In Surah An-Najm, the context in which it appears clarifies its entire meaning. He states:
(Does he have knowledge of the unseen, so he sees? Or has he not been informed of what was in the scriptures of Moses, and of Abraham, who fulfilled [his obligations]? That no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another) (An-Najm, 53:35-38).

Exhortation to the Prophet to remain firm in his call: 'fa-sbi-r' here implies the meaning of 'wait' (intadhir); hence, it is followed by the preposition 'li'. Finally, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is exhorted to remain firm and patient: stand firm and wait for the decision of your Lord, and avoid the haste shown by the companion of the fish (Jonah).
'The companion of the fish' refers to Prophet Jonah (peace be upon him). Addressing him by this title contains a form of affection, while referencing the trial he faced.
We have presented the details of Prophet Jonah's incident in its relevant place: when his nation showed disregard for his call, he was so affected by this insult to the truth that he departed without Allah's permission. For this, he was corrected, which resulted in the trial of the fish. Referencing this incident, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is exhorted: although your nation also persists in disregarding your call and rejecting you, stand firm in the field and wait for the decision of your Lord. Do not leave your place until your Lord commands, lest you face a trial similar to the one Prophet Jonah faced.
'when he called out while he was distressed' (idh nada wa huwa makdhum) - This points to the attitude Jonah chose after the trial. He immediately realized his mistake and, filled with intense distress, called out to his Lord from within the belly of the fish. The eternal words in which he called out are recorded in another place, and we have explained their eloquence. This is mentioned here to prevent any misunderstanding regarding Jonah because of the words 'do not be like the companion of the fish'; rather, it clarifies that although he committed a mistake under intense emotion, he immediately reformed it through repentance, and Allah blessed him with chosen status again, as detailed next.
_____
(There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers) (Al-Anbiya, 21:87).

'Ni'mah' (favor) here refers to the favor of Allah Almighty that he received after his repentance, in the form of acceptance of repentance and being recommissioned with the duty of messengership. It is stated: if he had not been overtaken by a favor from his Lord, he would have remained cast on the barren shore where the fish threw him, in a state of disgrace. But Allah accepted his repentance, blessed him with His mercy, chose him again to complete his sacred mission, and included him among the righteous. That is, he did not depart from this world as a failure, but was included among the successful—the righteous.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 49 and 50 was combined in the original text.]

This verse is also related to the exhortation of patience and firmness expressed in 'So be patient for the decision of your Lord'. That is: although the circumstances are extremely severe—when the disbelievers hear the Qur'an, they glare at you with such intense looks that it seems they would slip you from your position with their looks, and in their rage, they call you mad—stand firm in your position despite their attitude. Recall here the verse at the beginning of the Surah: (You are not, by the favor of your Lord, mad). The Surah ends with the same theme with which it began.

That is, if they call you mad upon hearing this, let them do so; but they should remember that this is not the braying of a madman, but a reminder for the worlds. If they do not benefit from this reminder, they will regret it, but that regret will be of no benefit.
